Seriously. I've got friends who are annoyed that this was ever made (and I'm 24, so I may just need friends who get it), but it is _so cool_ to see behind the scenes. Glad I'm not alone in thinking so.

​@masquerademageAbsolutely. If you watch and enjoy wrestling knowing that it's all just a fun and entertaining TV show, I don't see the problem with them letting everyone know how it's ran behind the scenes. It doesn't take away from the fact that the threat of injury is still very real. We all still know how dangerous it can be. But, we also know it's still scripted like a TV show. So really I don't see how it's hurting the lore. By the time I was like 12 or 13 I started to understand it wasn't real. So this wasn't a huge surprise to see haha. Ya know?

​@Clammiest For real! It's kinda like learning Santa isn't real. Like yeah, there goes your childhood, but now you can appreciate all the effort your family goes through to make Christmas a magical holiday.

Of course the characters are cool, and the storylines are fun to get invested in, but it's equally as cool to analyze the setup, and the people playing the characters and how their headspaces work, and recognize all the time and effort put in by talent and staff alike to keep the show rolling.

Now I'm just preaching to the choir, but I totally get it lol

​@masquerademageHaha for sure man! I love all that stuff. If anything, seeing all that stuff makes me feel even more fore the people playing the characters. Like, seeing Punk getting super emotional was cool. Or seeing Rhea having anxiety before matches. You never see that stuff. It makes you feel for them even more in a way. Idk haha.

Exposing the business is what ruined it, num nuts. I was in it. Back in the late 90s and early 2000s. The WWE was a billion-dollar corporation that drew more fans than the NBA, NFL, NHL, etc. Now they cannot come close to getting that many viewers. It was the same with Magic, when that masked illusionist exposed the tricks, everyone stopped watching; some things are better left to the imagination than being exposed to a bunch of clowns that have never watched it or made fun of it. This just caters to all those a$$holes that keep saying “that stuff’s fake” and “you’re a loser for watching that crap”. I almost became a cripple from a serious injury that still bothers me after 20 years or more. I hate Triple H and idiots like him that expose the business that they claim to love.

@timeknowsfilmsproductions978 No, Vince McMahon exposed the business in court in 94 to beat the steroid case. He pulled the Montreal Screwjob on live television, allowed the Curtain Call, and broke kayfabe in his declaration for the change to the Attitude Era in 97. And then WWF had the most prosperous era of its entire history. One NBC special exposing the secrets of wrestling didn’t ruin wrestling. What ruined wrestling was losing the 2 biggest stars of the attitude era within a year, the WCW buyout that has left WWE without viable competition to this day, and Vince McMahon getting complacent with the product and placating to shareholders and relying on TV rights deals more than the fans. Among a multitude of other reasons. You know where WWF was before McMahon “exposed the business”, heading in to one of the worst times in the company’s history and them almost shutting down and losing to WCW. Correlation doesn’t equal causation. The exposure of the business was happening due to the rise of the internet and it just wasn’t worth holding dear to the facade. So guess what numb nuts? I don’t really care if you were “in it” because I was, too. The fact of the matter is, we live in a different time and this content appeals to audiences and if WWE wants to put this content out then more power to them and the people that want to watch will, and those that don’t won’t watch. Just like the McMahon documentary that exposed 40 years of history, this is for the casual observers of wrestling that aren’t as fully invested as clearly either of us are.
